transcript.text: Hi, Saasha.
Hi.
Hi, how are you?
I'm fine in here.
Good, good.
So you're joining in from Durban then?
Yeah.
And you are with?
I am in Portugal right now.
So it's kind of sunny, kind of rainy, know, the weather's all over the place.
So, yeah.
I didn't know you were from South Africa.
was kind of praying you wouldn't have any load shedding going on.
yeah, actually, I'm the lucky few that doesn't have load shedding in my area.
So, yeah, I'm safe always.
Okay, good to know, good to know.
So, yeah, thank you.
Thank you for joining.
Thank you for taking the time to apply as well.
Pretty excited to get to know you better.
I took a look at your LinkedIn profile and some of these.
Articles you've written, et cetera.
I just wanted to dive into it.
They'll get to know your skillset better and give you a chance to explain what it's all about.
Just to get started, I just wanted to check.
It seems like, correct me if I'm wrong, but it seems like most of your experiences in healthcare writing slash SEO.
And I was just wondering, could you just tell me a little bit more about that?
How did you get into it?
How did that go, et cetera, and so on?
Okay, so I started, well, years ago, so like about 10 to 12 years ago, I started in healthcare.
So that was in nursing and in paramedics.
And I transitioned from being a healthcare worker to being in the background.
So that went into research and writing.
And then once I was there, I think I almost fell naturally into the other.
So like the other niches like marketing, business, SaaS, software, technology.
So it was like almost like a domino effect.
Like once I started, I just started going next, next, next, next.
Most of my experience, definitely because coming from healthcare or coming from healthcare writing, it's technical.
So technical writing, dissecting evidence and distilling different things or different concepts for the lay audience without interfering with accuracy, tone.
I think it's a lot of stuff that transitions well into business in general, business and marketing.
So, yeah, that's how I really got into it.
And for the past, I think four years now, four to five years, I've been in the business marketing, SaaS, software, AI, technology niche.
And, yeah, I've been enjoying it quite a lot.
And as I said, there's a lot of similarities there.
I've worked for companies across.
The UK, US, Australia, Europe.
So I've basically been pretty widespread, but I haven't worked for companies in South Africa, surprisingly.
So I'm from here, but no, I haven't worked for anyone here.
So I'm used to the global outreach, how you guys work and the work ethic, what you need to do, the individual deadline-driven mindset and also the team collaborative mindset.

And from writing, I naturally went into editing, quality analysis, and then it went more into, sometimes it's more like as time has been going by and AI has been coming in, I find myself more in hybrid roles now.
So it's like, what am I, I don't even know what to call myself.
I mean, I am a content writer, yes, but it seems like you're doing way more than just content writing.
You're trying to integrate your AI, you're trying to do your prompt engineering properly to get Fathom.
Streamline your results, streamline your content.
I mean, it's all over the place.
That's the moment.
Yeah, every day is different.
I think that mostly I'm just here to learn.
then when Marilla told me that it's more like a learning role, and although I'm a content writer, it comes in clutch with wanting to know more, expand yourself.
So I think that's what got me here with you guys.
Awesome.
Thank you.
Thank you for that.
Lots of detail.
Really appreciate it.
I do want to touch on your AI experience a little bit later.
But before that, I just want to clarify, since, you know, the reason, one of the reasons I asked about the healthcare experience is, was it more SEO oriented?
I'm seeing some of your samples on, there's the Clarity, there's one on Health Today as well.
It seems to be more SEO oriented.
Is that a correct assessment?
Okay.
Yeah.
So, whereas I think my new image medical work, which is, isn't live because I was a ghost writer.
I'm sorry.
So I don't think that one's showing up there.
But with those companies, yeah, it was very SEO driven.
So it's where you are balancing your SEO with your quality of your articles.
So trying to just get on first page, but also give the readers what they need, what they want, anticipate their needs.
So yeah, that kind of thing.
So SEO, very strong in what I do, basically.
I did have one weird question about the Clarity.
They featured you as, you know, obviously they featured your name in the, what do you call it, as a byline.
But in your author page in Clarity, they have you as Shisha Govender for some reason.
Yeah, so yeah, they did change that at one point, when I did change my name.
So due to religious seasons, I went from Shisha to Saasha.
Ah, okay, okay.
I was just wondering.
Yeah, because I saw Shisha show up, I was like, okay, is this a sister or okay?
No, Okay.
Well, thank you.
was just a weird quote I was wondering about.
Okay, regarding the SEO itself, I'm sure you've been doing this for a while, for close to eight years now.
Do you have any sort of opinions on how you would capture the snippet, for example?
Any thoughts on that?
Just keen to hear what you think of that.
Like, if I were to come here and say, like, okay, I have this FAQ article or something, and I want some of these to show up in the snippet or in the AI overviews now, but let's say a snippet.
How would you sort of think through that?
How would you go about doing something like that?
So, from like an SEO standpoint?
Yes.
Okay.
So, I think, well, mostly I work with SEO teams.
like, the actual SEO keywords and the density or the phrases and the density came to me, but I also have been working.
So I think with me, my process is really backwards.
mean, it doesn't really work for most people, but I tend to work from the bottom up.
So once I have my keywords, I tend to do the research and then look at the quality of what I really want out there.
So let's say if I was saying this business is about logs or cutting logs or timber, for example.
I take the most relevant SEO keywords that I came up with or whatever Surfer gives me or Astros gives me.
And I work around that to build the article and then inject quality into it.
I think SEO is very important, but it shouldn't be taken.
I think it shouldn't be first preference because a lot of time when you put SEO as first preference, I've noticed working with a lot of companies, they force you to do that.
And yes, your name is behind.

But they forced you to put the SEO first, and the quality really drops, then you're getting bounce rates, nobody really wants to visit that site, your quality of your information is dropping drastically.
So for me, it's always a balance.
So that's how I would really work it out.
Okay.
So just to continue that thought, I'm glad you brought it up.

When you say content quality, what exactly, let's say you're an editor and you're given an article, do you have any sort of go-to hacks or something that you use to quickly figure out that, okay, this is a quality piece, this is not a quality piece, any shortcuts you use or any methods you use?
I would think my shortcuts go mainly from my client briefs.
So whatever brief the client gave or the initial thoughts, the initial comments about what they wanted for their content, I usually make a checklist out of that.
So my checklist is there, and that's how I look.
I quickly, like I...
As you said, like a shortcut to see is this quality, isn't this quality.
If you can hit what the client wants in the first, I would say, first 300 words of your article, you're on the right page.
And then maybe expand as you're going.
Or at least build up to that point to where you want, where the client wants to be.
So I think, yeah, that's my shortcut.
Basically taking my client notes, taking my client's brief, and going from there.

Thereafter, go with style, tone, competitor analysis, and then check on those points to see are we working with quality here or are we just working with plain keyword stuffing and things that really don't make sense.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Good point about keyword stuffing, for sure.
It is something, you know, I just realized I never introduced myself.
I should have done this up front.
I'm so sorry.
My position here is.
I'm a senior managing editor at Growthx, so a lot of my job basically involves editing.
work with people in the account manager role, basically reviewing a lot of work, and there's a lot of back and forth between me and the writers.
So these kinds of things often come up.
That's why I'm asking you these questions.
Going back to the brief itself, what if you weren't given a brief?
Because as part of our day-to-day, what has usually happened is we have, let's say, an article title.
It's a basic, you can say, some sort of an idea.
You have the SEO research that is kind of done for you.
You're given the keywords, etc.
You're given a sort of a two-sentence overview of what this article should be about.
That's basically your brief.
You can add the keywords in there.
Is that enough for you to go off of?
Do you need a little bit more from that?
I just keep to hear your process.
Okay.
I think there's definitely more than enough to go off of.
When you get that few sentences and you get your keywords, and as I said, I think that goes well with what I said before, where I get my keyword list and then I'm building around the keyword list.
So once you have those things, I would say, obviously, we know our client's name and their business.
So look at the competitors.
Who's coming up on first page?
What's the best content out there?
And then use all of that data or that insight to build an article that would probably surpass what the competitors have done already.
So, yeah, I think that would be enough to work on.
And how do you, sorry, yeah, thank you.
And how would you, well, let's talk about differentiation.
I'm curious about one of your articles here.
I'm referring to one of your clarity articles, which is the sleeping positions for pain.
I noticed this tends to come up a lot because even in the health today, you had a couple articles kind.
No similar, which was around sleeping positions.
I'm just wondering, I haven't looked at the data, but this seems to be popular search.
There's a lot of articles about this.
Just keen to get your thoughts.
How would you go about differentiating something like this?
Let's say I come to you and I say, I'm the editor at Clarity, or I'm coming to you.
I need an article on sleeping positions and pain, etc.
You do your SEO research.
How would you think about differentiation?
Just want to hear you think it out loud.
There's no right or wrong answer.
Okay.
I think definitely it's like the out of the box thinking and that is really like a widespread thing.
As you said, there's no right or wrong answer.
Everyone has a different way of out of the box thinking.
I'd say approach.
So if everyone is approaching the sleeping, like the sleeping positions and going straight for the position itself, you can go from the lifestyle.
Angle.
So how does my lifestyle affect my sleeping positions and then move from there?

So basically take the opposite angle, an angle nobody thought of, an angle that hasn't been seen, at least hasn't been seen for your first five or six competitors that you've seen come up on your first page.
So if they haven't done it yet, if you're going to do it, I think it's going to draw in more of an audience and make the audience think that you are the difference or that silver lining.
Yeah.
So I would say go from a different angle and then answer another question that's related to it.
That's a great answer.
Thank you.
What about examining the audience itself?
Do you think there's a way to differentiate there?
Say, I don't know, because we do a lot of B2B writing, right?
All of our clients are B2B.
So there's multiple people reading it.
So for example, one of the accounts that my pod manages.
We address people like data engineers, address people like senior data leaders, we address business analysts and data analysts.
We have like, you know, four ICPs, basically.
Do you think there's a way to, you know, just building on the angle that you mentioned, do you think there's a way to differentiate based on the audience?
Like, how would you think about that?
Like, if I asked you, okay, this article is for data engineers, I need to make it for business analysts.
What would be your thought process?
Again, no wrong answers, just trying to think out loud.
Okay.
I'd say if we know, like you said, your audience is the data engineer.
So we know basically the scope or the field we're going in.
So we're not going to plunge into like this wide variety of people.
So I think we'd be more distinct with what we're doing.
So we would know of research, what data engineers really want to know about a certain topic, what's the top five things they want to know.
And move from the opposite angle using that data there.
So that research that you collected.
And how would you collect that research?
Sorry to interrupt.
No.
Yeah, you were saying?
How would you sort of go about collecting that research?
Like what would your process there be?
I think my process would be a bit, okay, this may be unorthodox.
I'm not sure, but as you just said, our main thing is data engineers.
So I'm going to go just like how I'm doing keywords, I'm going to go engineers, so-and-so, and type it straight into Google and see where I fly off from there.
Because you're most probably going to get a very streamlined result of exactly what you need.
And fast.
We can't go too slow with this.
And on the other end, you have chat GPT, which is really helpful.
And with the right prompts, I think it gives you exactly what you need.
So yeah, I think that would be fine.
Thank
I'm glad you mentioned that.
What sort of prompts do you think you would feed into GPT when you're doing this kind of research?
Okay, so I first give it a bit of insight.
So say what article I really need to create and what research I'm really looking for so it can streamline my results.

So I think be concise, be clear, and don't leave anything up to interpretation because machine learning, I mean, they are quite intelligent, but when you leave things up to interpretation, they seem to veer off in all the wrong ends.
So yeah, I would say just be concise with what I'm saying.
So if it's data engineers, my article is about data engineers.
I'm writing for so-and-so thing, and this is what I'm looking for.
So all I want them to give me is basically that core information that I'm looking for.
Okay, interesting.
It's funny you say that.
We do something quite similar, but we do it on purpose.
complexity as well.
How do you use AI these days?
You mentioned you're using it a lot.
How are you using it in terms of writing, et cetera?
Okay, so at the moment, like I do a lot of freelance work, so it's mostly in technology software.
And I think I'm using it exactly how I said.
So if I have a topic, I just put in my concise, my article title, about two lines about what my article is going to be about.
Sometimes I ask, who do you think my target audience is going to be, and who am I not targeting?
And that's where I get my different approaches from different angles.
So that's how I'm using it these days, to just get my briefs, basically, to get my outline of my article.
And then I flesh it out from there.
Okay, nice.
And since, you know, just a question, you've been freelancing for a long time.
How do you feel about working on a team, cetera?
Because it's quite a transition.
is it's I've I'm just you it's on little bit information, I'm
I just want to get your thoughts on that.
Well, freelancing at the moment, not by choice.
So I was pretty solid on a team for a very long time.
think it was almost five, six years.
And then the company actually decided to just close up ranks a bit on certain sections of them.
So, yeah, so that's why I started freelancing.
I love working in a team.
love consistency.
I think that when you work in a team, the each day environment thing is really interesting.
Versus freelancing, it's almost like if you're on your own.
I mean, most of the time it gets pretty lonely.
And all we have to talk to is Chachi, which is getting pretty boring around here.
So I think I'll work pretty well integrating into a team or transitioning into that.
Okay.
And just to round off the SEO thing.
Do you have experience at all building reports?
Do
Anything like in Looker or something like that?
Or are you proficient in reading those reports in Search Console or anything like that?
No, not at the moment.
I think when I was doing reports, it was more like manually doing reports.
So it was SOPs that I came up with and then my quality analysis, how I wanted to do it with a team of writers and then checked up on that and gave the quality feedback using that.
So no software use, but definitely open to learning and click on the trigger with that, with the software and reading and all of that.
Awesome.
And just real quick, obviously, you know, there was a reason you applied to growthx.
Is there something you're looking to learn and upskill in some way?
Like, is there, you know, just curious about if there's that sort of an angle here?
Okay, I would say upskill on...
So basically, at the moment, I still see myself.
I mean, I've been here for a lot of years and I have been an editor and a quality assessor before, but I still see myself more of a content writer.
And I really want to go up a few notches from that.
And I feel like the opportunity to learn has been there.
And although you're eager, it's sometimes to really push yourself into that intersection is not happening.
And when I saw you guys, I think that intersection was there.
And I said, well, let's give it a chance.
Because sometimes you can be really eager, but the doors are shuffling really fast before you even get in there.
Yeah.
So I think that's one of my things to upskill on all of the cylinders.
Yeah.
And then learn a widespread of things.
Okay.
Like what?
So as you just mentioned about the report reading and the software, I would really like to learn more about that.
I'd learn.
I'd like to learn how to use AI and technology in a more streamlined way, in a more structured way, where you can get your results faster.
Workflow management, I'd like to learn management in general.
I would say upskilling myself in my writing.
Yes, I can write.
Yes, I know how to do technical writing and creative writing, but there's so much more to that.
Every company brings a different feel, a different thing to learn.
I think no matter how many years you're in a company, you can't know enough.
Everywhere has a different process, and in learning each process, you really expand yourself in your knowledge and your skill.
I'm also looking for somewhere to set down roots.
I think I've been freelancing for a while, and as I said, not by choice, and I really don't want to do that anymore.
I want to be part of a team and the growth, and to say five years from now, okay, I started off here, and this is where I am now.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Okay.
Thank for that.
That was all the questions I had for you.
Do you have any for me that I can clarify?
No, none at the moment.
I think Marilla actually gave me a lot of the core information that I needed to know when we first met.
Yeah, so nothing at the moment.
Okay.
I just want to confirm in case Marilla didn't sort of do this, like we do have the four-hour overlap with specific time.
Is that okay for you?
Yeah, definitely okay with me.
As I said, I've been working globally for a while now, so I'm used to like switching up my time zones.
And yeah, I'm not going to fall asleep on you guys.
Good to know.
Good to know.
The salary which was mentioned in the job description, is that for your expectations?
Okay.
And then the...
If we were to offer you the position, is there any notice period you need, what would be a start date, et cetera?
As I was telling her, think I'd need a week.
Yeah, like a week or so.
Or if you guys like, maybe you'd like to start a new month fresh, I'm fine with that as well.
Okay, great.
And just the last thing, in terms of, do you have experience in managing multiple accounts?
You know, obviously you've been a freelancer as well, managing multiple deadlines, managing multiple requests and expectations.
Like, how do you sort of go about doing that?
What's your process over there?
Yeah, I do have experience managing multiple accounts.
As a freelancer and when I was working, like, pretty firmly with another company, I think organization is key.
Literally try to organize myself by priority and deadlines.
highest priority first, closest deadlines go on the top.
And I do it digitally and the old-fashioned pen and paper way.
So as my days are going, I'm literally ticking off my tasks.
I'm ticking off who, what, where, and then also doing it digitally.
I'm being reminded constantly, like alarm bells going off saying, okay, you need to do this, you need to do that.
So for me, organization is a big thing.
Okay, nice.
And, well, does that organization, how do you prefer communicating on, obviously since it's a remote company, we do everything on Slack.
How do you prefer communication on there?
Is it, you know, do you need like constant reminders of work or do you need like constant check-ins or daily check-ins or how does, what does that look like?
Well, I've been working with Slack as well, with most of my companies for the past, I don't know how many years.
is you
I'm quite used to working on Slack and being, it's almost like another WhatsApp to me.
I'm checking it constantly and seeing where I need to be.
So yeah, I'm pretty used to that.
And with the check-ins, I don't mind like a week, a daily or a weekly check-in.
I think in the beginning, that's fine with me.
What I don't like is like every hour, if you're like on my back saying, do you have this?
Do you have this?
Like not really giving you time to think about anything or creatively do anything.
You're just like pestering somebody.
I think that's, yeah, that's one of my pet peeves there.
But with the rest, the weekly month, I mean, the weekly reminders or the daily check-ins I'm fine with.
And Slackview is totally fine with.
Okay, great.
Thank you so much, Sasha, for this.
It was really, I enjoyed talking to you very much and definitely agreed with you on many points about content quality and, you know, minimizing the AI hallucinations.
Yeah.
you.
Especially as well.
So it was great.
thank you so much for taking the time.
The next step is I will sort of, we have an internal process, so I will go in there and enter, you know, everything that we spoke about.
And then Marilia will get back in touch with you with regards to the next steps.
Okay.
Thank you.
All right.
Thank you.
Thank you very much.
Have a nice day.
You too.
Bye.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Interview with Saasha Govender for a content writing position at growthx.ai](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=1.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Saasha has extensive experience in healthcare, SEO, and technical writing, with a recent focus on business, marketing, SaaS, and AI content](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=2.0)
  - [She emphasizes balancing SEO with content quality, using client briefs and competitor analysis to guide her work](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=11.0)
  - [Saasha is eager to upskill, particularly in areas like report analysis, AI integration, and management](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=248.0)
  - [She's comfortable with remote work, Slack communication, and managing multiple accounts/deadlines](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=1493.0)

## Topics

### Professional Background and Transition

  - [Started in healthcare (nursing, paramedics) 10-12 years ago](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=96.0)
  - [Transitioned to research and writing, then expanded to marketing, business, SaaS, software, and technology](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=107.0)
  - [4-5 years of experience in business/marketing/tech content](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=163.0)
  - [Worked with companies globally (UK, US, Australia, Europe)](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=180.0)
  - [Evolved from writing to editing, quality analysis, and hybrid roles incorporating AI](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=207.0)

### SEO and Content Quality Approach

  - [Balances SEO requirements with content quality](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=306.0)
  - [Process: Start with keywords, conduct research, then inject quality](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=442.0)
  - [Prioritizes meeting client needs in the first 300 words](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=309.0)
  - [Uses client briefs, style guidelines, and competitor analysis to assess quality](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=526.0)
  - [For differentiation, suggests taking unique angles (e.g., lifestyle approach for sleep positions)](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=758.0)
  - [Adapts content for specific audiences (e.g., data engineers vs. business analysts)](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=660.0)

### AI Integration in Writing Process

  - [Uses AI (like ChatGPT) for research and article outlines](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=949.0)
  - [Emphasizes clear, concise prompts to get targeted information](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=972.0)
  - [Open to learning more about streamlined AI usage in content creation](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=1253.0)

### Team Integration and Career Goals

  - [Prefers team environment over freelancing](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=1104.0)
  - [Seeking consistency and daily team interactions](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=1117.0)
  - [Aims to upskill in areas like report analysis, AI integration, and management](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=1253.0)
  - [Looking for long-term growth opportunities and to "set down roots" with a company](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=1302.0)

### Work Style and Communication Preferences

  - [Organized approach to managing multiple accounts and deadlines](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=1427.0)
  - [Uses both digital and paper-based task management systems](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=1440.0)
  - [Comfortable with Slack for communication](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=1493.0)
  - [Prefers daily or weekly check-ins, not hourly micromanagement](https://fathom.video/share/yDRSm2_5yUkp1gwZfxd2fnS6SgLxuYGx?tab=summary&timestamp=1510.0)
